nameMeanings.name: De
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Dilip, Anirban, Gopal, Alok, Anindya, Arunava, Asit, Asok, Dibyendu, Goutam, Indra, Indranil.
Indian: variant of   Dey  .

variants.name: Dey
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Indian Ashit, Indu, Naina, Partha, Soumya, Ajit, Anjan, Anjana, Anup, Arjun, Arun, Ashim.
English, Scottish, and Welsh: variant of   Day  .
German: topographic name from a field name in North Rhine-Westphalia, denoting a sizeable piece of land, from Middle Low German dē ‘thigh’ (referring to the shape of the property).

nameMeanings.name: Sainte
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Variant of   Sain   1, a surname of German origin.
French: probably a variant of Saïn (see Sain 6).
West African (Gambia): variant of the Serer name Sène (see Sene 2).

variants.name: Sain
meanings: Americanized form of German Zirn, which is a variant of Zürn (see Zurn ). Compare Saine , Sane , and Sayne .

Croatian and Serbian (Šain): metonymic occupational name for a hawker or a nickname from ša(h)in ‘falcon, hawk (trained for hunting)’, a word of Turkish and ultimately Persian origin (see Sahin ). Compare Shain and Shine .

Americanized form of Slovenian and Croatian Šajn: nickname from šajn, an obsolete loanword from German meaning ‘shine’, or a Slavicized form of German Schein . As a Croatian surname it may also be a variant of Šain (see 2 above). Compare Shain and Shine .

variants.name: Sene
meanings: French (Sené and Séné): nickname from Old French sené ‘wise, sensible’.

West African (Senegal; Sène): name found among the Serer people, of unexplained etymology. The same name forms part of the name of Roog Seen, the supreme deity in the indigenous Serer religion. Compare Saine 3.

nameMeanings.name: Barbe
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Emile, Laurent, Andre, Gillis, Jean Pierre, Serge, Sylvie, Thierry, Yves.
French: descriptive nickname for someone with a beard, Old French barbe (from Latin barba).
French (Barbé): adjectival derivative of 1, meaning ‘bearded’.

variants.name: Barb
meanings: German: variant of   Barbe  . This surname is also found in France (mainly Alsace).
Romanian: variant of   Barbu  .

variants.name: Barbier
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Dominique, Emile, Pierre, Alexandre, Gilles, Jacques, Laurent, Laurette, Manon, Marcel, Marcellin, Michel.
French: occupational name for a barber-surgeon (see Barber ), Old French barbier (from Late Latin barbarius, a derivative of barba ‘beard’). Compare Barbe and Barbee .
German: occupational name for a barber, from Middle High German barbierer, or an occupational name for an armorer, from barbier(e) ‘metal visor (to protect the face)’.

variants.name: Barbon
meanings: English: habitational name from Barbon in Westmorland. The placename is probably from Old Norse bjórr ‘beaver’ + brunnr ‘stream’, alternatively from Old English bera ‘bear’ + burna ‘stream’.
English: nickname from Middle English bare bon ‘bare bone’, perhaps denoting a thin or bare-legged man.
French and English (of French Huguenot origin): nickname for a bearded man, from an augmentative of French   Barbe  .

variants.name: Barby
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Merwin, Otto, Wolfgang.  French Celestine, Pierre.
German: habitational name from a place so named in Saxony-Anhalt.
French (mainly Dordogne): derivative of   Barbe   1.

variants.name: Barbin
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Angelle, Marcel.
French: diminutive of   Barbe   1.

variants.name: Barbot
meanings: French:
from the Old French female personal name Barbot, a diminutive of Barbe , a short form of Barbara .
nickname for someone who tended to speak indistinctly, from French barboter ‘to babble’, or for someone with a beard, from a diminutive of Old French barbe ‘beard’.

variants.name: Barbay
meanings: French: variant of Barbé (see Barbe ).

nameMeanings.name: Robert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the many French names of Germanic origin that were introduced into Britain by the Normans; it has since remained in continuous use. It is derived from the nearly synonymous elements hrōd ‘fame’ + berht ‘bright, famous’, and had a native Old English predecessor of similar form (Hreodbeorht), which was supplanted by the Norman name. Two dukes of Normandy in the 11th century bore the name: the father of William the Conqueror (sometimes identified with the legendary Robert the Devil), and his eldest son. It was borne also by three kings of Scotland, notably Robert the Bruce ( 1274–1329 ), who freed Scotland from English domination. The altered short form Bob is very common, but Hob and Dob, which were common in the Middle Ages and gave rise to surnames, are extinct. See also Rupert .
